message.content:
Explore the IDAHO-VAULT repository at c:\Users\loganf\Documents\IDAHO-VAULT. I need to understand:

1. What's in the `.qodo` directory — list all files and read their contents
2. Read the file `Qodo - Documentation - Migrating to Qodo v2.md` if it exists at the root
3. Check if there's a `.venv` directory or `requirements.txt` that might relate to a Python virtual environment being activated in the terminal
4. Check `VAULT-CONVENTIONS.md` in the `!` directory for any Qodo-related configuration

Return all file contents you find.

content.text: Perfect! Now let me read the key files:

content.content:
1	---
2	source: "https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/migrating-to-qodo-v2"
3	author:
4	  - "[[Qodo Documentation]]"
5	published:
6	created: 2026-03-29
7	---
8	Starting February 4, 2026, new Qodo accounts will not have access to the Qodo v1 code review experience.
9	
10	This guide explains how you can transition from Qodo v1 to Qodo v2’s new code review experience safely and incrementally. The goal is to let you test and validate the new review behavior before enabling it broadly across your organization. To use the new code review experience, you update your repository configuration and enable new review commands. During migration, you can run the new review alongside existing commands for testing purposes, then remove duplicates once validation is complete.
11	
12	### Step 1: Update your repository configuration
13	
14	Configuration for code review lives in your repository’s [configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview). In the `.pr_agent.toml` you can see which review commands are enabled and how feedback is generated.
15	
16	#### Add the new review commands
17	
18	In the `git_provider` section (varies depending on your provider), add the new review commands (prefixed with `/agentic`):
19	
20	```shellscript
21	⚠️ Select ONLY ONE section below — the one that matches your git provider.
22	# Do not include multiple provider sections in the same configuration file.
23	
24	# GitHub App
25	[github_app]
26	pr_commands = [
27	  "/agentic_describe",
28	  "/agentic_review"
29	]
30	
31	# GitLab
32	[gitlab]
33	pr_commands = [
34	  "/agentic_describe",
35	  "/agentic_review"
36	]
37	
38	# Bitbucket Cloud / App
39	[bitbucket_app]
40	pr_commands = [
41	  "/agentic_describe",
42	  "/agentic_review"
43	]
44	
45	# Bitbucket Server / Data Center
46	[bitbucket_server]
47	pr_commands = [
48	  "/agentic_describe",
49	  "/agentic_review"
50	]
51	
52	# Azure DevOps Server
53	[azure_devops_server]
54	pr_commands = [
55	  "/agentic_describe",
56	  "/agentic_review"
57	]
58	```
59	
60	The new commands combine and replace multiple legacy commands, including `/improve` `/review` `/describe` & `/compliance`
61	
62	During testing, both old and new review commands may run in parallel for comparison, but once migration is complete, older commands should be removed to avoid duplicate feedback and noise.
63	
64	**Optional: Test the new review manually** Manual testing is an optional way to preview the new code review experience before or during configuration. In any pull request comment, enter:
65	
66	```shellscript
67	/agentic_describe 
68	/agentic_review
69	```
70	
71	This enables you to:
72	- Validate the new review output on real pull requests
73	- Compare results with existing review behavior
74	- Test without changing repository configuration
75	
76	Manual invocation is intended for testing and validation.
77	
78	### Step 2: Enable on a pilot repository
79	
80	Before rolling out the new code review experience across your organization, we recommend enabling it on a pilot repository. This helps you:
81	- Evaluate signal quality and noise levels
82	- Confirm the new review behavior meets expectations
83	- Adjust configuration if needed
84	- Remove duplicate commands after validation
85	**Configuration details** To learn how to configure options such as severity thresholds, display modes, verbosity levels, and ignore rules, see [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals.
86	
87	### Step 3: Enable on entire organization
88	
89	After you’ve validated the new code review experience in a pilot repository, you can roll it out across your organization by applying the configuration at the organization level. To enable the new review commands across all repositories, define them in the **global organization configuration**: After this change is merged, the configuration applies as the default for all repositories in the organization (unless overridden by a repo-level or wiki configuration).
90	
91	### Step 4: Remove Qodo v1 configuration
92	
93	Once you enable the new commands broadly, remove older review commands from any configuration location where they are still defined (wiki, repo-level, or global). See [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals for more details.
94	
95	## What you’ll notice in your PRs
96	
97	After rollout, review output will be consolidated and more structured:
98	- Compliance and Code Suggestions will no longer appear as separate outputs (they are incorporated into the new review experience)
99	- Issues are grouped by priority (for example, Action Required vs Remediation Recommended)
100	- Findings include clearer explanations and direct references to relevant code
101	- Reviews include agent-assisted fix prompts to help resolve issues faster
102	These changes are expected and reflect a more coordinated and prioritized review flow.

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1 # VAULT-CONVENTIONS — Shared Reference for All Agents 2 3 This file contains the vault conventions shared by all AI agents working in IDAHO-VAULT. Individual agent instructions (`CLAUDE.md`, `.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `GEMINI.md`) reference this file for vault structure, naming, frontmatter, and protocol. 4 5 **Owner:** Logan Finney — journalist, producer/reporter, Idaho Reports / Idaho Public Television 6 **Repository:** github.com/loganfinney27/IDAHO-VAULT (public) 7 **Platform:** Obsidian.md vault, version-controlled with git 8 9 --- 10 11 ## Vault Purpose 12 13 This is a personal journalism research vault. It contains notes on Idaho politics, government, legislation, people, organizations, and source documents. All committed content is **on the record** and should be treated as **publishable**. 14 15 --- 16 17 ## Vault Structure 18 19 This vault is intentionally hybrid. Governance and automation live in dedicated 20 system folders, while a large share of the journalism corpus lives directly at 21 repo root. Do not assume that a root-heavy layout means the vault is 22 "unorganized," and do not use older taxonomy examples as permission to 23 restructure the current vault. 24 25 ### Root Folder Semantics 26 27 | Path | Meaning | Agent rule | 28 | --- | --- | --- | 29 | `!/` | Canonical governance, protocols, levelsets, records, routing | Read first for system truth. Do not restructure, rename, or clean without Logan's explicit direction. | 30 | Repo root `.md` files | Primary note corpus and working knowledge base | Root-flat notes are intentional. Do not mass-move them into category folders without explicit authorization. | 31 | `INBOX/` | Intake and staging area | Use for intake only when directed. Do not auto-empty or normalize it. | 32 | `swarm/` | Swarm support artifacts and structured coordination data | Treat as infrastructure, not general note taxonomy. | 33 | `.github/` | Automation, workflows, scripts, and GitHub-specific instructions | Safe to modify only within assigned automation work and governance boundaries. | 34 | `.obsidian/` | Obsidian application configuration | Not note content. Respect sync and git boundaries before changing anything here. | 35 | Agent/persona dotfolders such as `.claude/`, `.codex/`, `.gemini/`, `.grok/`, `.deepseek/`, `.google/`, `.meta/`, `.microsoft/`, `.perplexity/`, `.persephone/`, `.zagreus/`, `.bartimaeus/` | Agent/persona shims, governance files, and local identity infrastructure | Protected. Do not delete, rename, consolidate, or "clean up" these folders unless it is your own dotfolder or Logan explicitly directs the change. | 36 | Tooling folders such as `.venv/`, `.vscode/`, `.qodo/` | Local environment or tool support | Do not infer that a hidden folder is disposable just because it is small, empty, or unfamiliar. | 37 38 ### Folder Rules For Emerging Agents 39 40 - Treat `!/` as the vault control plane. 41 - Treat root-flat notes as a deliberate operating choice, not a mistake to fix. 42 - Treat persona dotfolders as keystone infrastructure, even when they contain 43 only a shim file or appear empty. 44 - If a folder's purpose is unclear, stop and ask Logan before proposing 45 deletion, consolidation, or mass moves. 46 - Historical references to older folder trees are descriptive context, not 47 standing authorization to reorganize the live vault. 48 49 --- 50 51 ## Naming Conventions 52 53 | Type | Pattern | Example | 54 | -------------- | -------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- | 55 | Bills | `(YYYY) Bill Type Number.md` | `(2026) House Bill 24.md` | 56 | News articles | `YYYY-MM-DD - Outlet - Title.md` | `2024-01-15 - Idaho Statesman - Title here.md` | 57 | Hearings | `YYYY-MM-DD - Committee or Meeting.md` | `2023-12-19 - GIAC meeting.md` | 58 | People | `Full Name.md` | `Brad Little.md` | 59 | Other entities | Descriptive name, title case | `Ada County.md` | 60 61 --- 62 63 ## Frontmatter Conventions 64 65 All Obsidian files use YAML frontmatter. The canonical header/footer policy is defined in `!/VAULT-METADATA-STANDARD.md` and should be treated as the source of truth for required fields, optional fields, lifecycle status, timestamp format, authorship, and authority. 66 67 ### Baseline Required Fields (all governed markdown notes) 68 69 ```yaml 70 title: "" 71 updated: YYYY-MM-DD 72 status: 73 authority: "" 74 ``` 75 76 ### Type-Specific Additions 77 78 Tags are stored in frontmatter only. Treat `tags:` as the canonical tag source for a note, use lowercase slash-path tags, and keep date/session/election tags as tags when they are part of the note taxonomy. 79 80 **People:** 81 82 ```yaml 83 tags: 84 - party/republican # or party/democratic 85 - people/elected/legislative 86 residence: "[[Boise]]" 87 ``` 88 89 **News articles:** 90 91 ```yaml 92 author: "[[Reporter Name]]" 93 outlet: "[[Outlet Name]]" 94 URL: https://... 95 tags: 96 - media/articles 97 - 2024/01/15 98 ``` 99 100 **Bills:** 101 102 ```yaml 103 tags: 104 - bills 105 - 2026/session 106 aliases: 107 - HB 24 108 cmte: ["[[Committee Name]]"] 109 sponsor: ["[[Sponsor Name]]"] 110 URL: https://legislature.idaho.gov/... 111 ``` 112 113 **Hearings:** 114 115 ```yaml 116 cmte: "[[Committee Name]]" 117 tags: 118 - 2023/12/19 119 ``` 120 121 --- 122 123 ## Wikilinks 124 125 Use `[[Full Name]]` for all internal links — people, places, organizations, bills, topics. This is how Obsidian builds the knowledge graph. Link densely in source documents. 126 127 --- 128 129 ## File Types 130 131 - **Markdown** = human product, attributable to Logan. Notes, stories, analysis. 132 - **Python** = machine/procedural product, attributable to AI agents. Scripts, scrapers, automation. 133 - **Administrative** = vault infrastructure. Instruction files, audit reports. 134 135 --- 136 137 ## Vault ↔ Linear Operating Model Mapping 138 139 | Layer (purpose) | Vault (canonical memory) | Linear (execution state) | Chat/Slack (ephemeral) | 140 | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------- | 141 | **Core / Mind**
governing doctrine | Constitution, Charter, Codex, decision logs, doctrine/guidance updates, LEVELSET snapshots | Work items to draft/revise doctrine; link PRs/issues to vault artifacts | Quick clarifications; capture any decision back into Vault/PR issue | 142 | **Periphery / Body**
operating mechanics | Protocols, procedures, preferences, templates/SOPs, stable checklists, automation docs | Tasks/epics for doing the work, tracking status, ownership, deadlines | Live coordination, handoffs, Q&A; move outcomes to Linear/Vault | 143 | **Ghost / Soul**
interpretive/cultural layer | Guidelines, grimoire/interpretive notes, guestbook/cultural norms, context vaults | Action items emerging from interpretive work (e.g., follow-ups, retro tasks) | Brainstorms, vibes, drafts; memorialize decisions/insights in Vault | 144 | **`!` spaces**
operational infrastructure | System files, DOCKET, LEVELSET, agent routing, audit/log artifacts, workflow outputs that must persist | Incidents/infra tasks, runbooks in execution, workflow status, tickets | Real-time paging/alerts; record outcomes in Linear and Vault | 145 146 **Decision Rule:** Vault holds doctrine and context that must persist. Linear tracks execution, owners, and current state. Chat/Slack is transient coordination — any decision or durable context must be promoted promptly into Vault and/or Linear. 147 148 --- 149 150 ## Automation 151 152 ### Active Automation Scripts 153 154 | Script | Purpose | Trigger | 155 | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | 156 | `sort_audit.py` | Audits vault structure for misplaced files | Weekly Monday 6 AM UTC + manual | 157 | `idaho_leg_scraper.py` | Scrapes Idaho Legislature bill data | Daily 6 AM MT + manual | 158 | `post_digest.py` | Posts bill activity to GitHub Issues digest | Called by scraper workflow | 159 | `propose_moves.py` | Proposes vault file reorganization | Weekly Monday 7 AM UTC + manual | 160 | `wayback_audit.py` | Audits URL preservation in Wayback Machine | Weekly Monday 8 AM UTC + manual | 161 | `daily_rollover.py` | Rolls over daily note tasks | Daily 4 AM MT | 162 | `linear_brief_generator.py` | Generates research briefs from Linear issues | Called by linear-brief workflow | 163 | `classify_paths.py` | Classifies changed files by risk tier | Called by auto-pr workflow | 164 | `validate_content.py` | Validates vault content structure | Called by multiple workflows | 165 | `post_levelset_closure.py` | Notifies when LEVELSET files ready for closure | Called by levelset-closure workflow | 166 167 ### Utility Scripts (Manual Use Only) 168 169 These scripts are not called by automated workflows but are available for manual vault maintenance: 170 171 | Script | Purpose | Usage | 172 | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------------------------------- | 173 | `expand_date_aliases.py` | One-off: expands date alias frontmatter in daily notes | `python3 .github/scripts/expand_date_aliases.py [--dry-run]` | 174 | `normalize_tags.py` | Normalizes Markdown note tags across vault | `python3 .github/scripts/normalize_tags.py [--write]` | 175 | `tidy_daily_notes.py` | One-off: normalizes daily note frontmatter structure | `python3 .github/scripts/tidy_daily_notes.py [--dry-run]` | 176 | `obsidian_rest_api_client.py` | REST API client for Obsidian Local REST API plugin | Import/use in other scripts as needed | 177 | `mcp_guardrails.py` | MCP protocol guardrails (reserved for future MCP integration) | Import/use in MCP-enabled scripts | 178 179 Scripts live in `.github/scripts/`. Workflows live in `.github/workflows/`. Scripts that commit to the repo use `git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"`. Dependencies are tracked in `.github/scripts/requirements-scraper.txt`. 180 181 ### MCP Action Logging Requirement (Mandatory) 182 183 Any automation in `.github/workflows/` or `.github/scripts/` that performs an MCP-mediated action **must** emit a structured log entry using the following reusable template. 184 185 #### Required MCP Action Log Template 186 187 ```yaml 188 mcp_action_log: 189 action_type: "" 190 system_or_resource_id: "" 191 initiating_agent: "" 192 correlation_id: "" 193 outcome: "" 194 retry_count: 195 related_ref: "" 196 ``` 197 198 #### Field Definitions 199 200 - `action_type`: The MCP operation category (for example: `read_resource`, `write_resource`, `invoke_tool`). 201 - `system_or_resource_id`: The MCP server/system identifier or concrete resource identifier targeted by the action. 202 - `initiating_agent`: Agent identity that initiated the MCP action (for example: `agent:codex`, `agent:claude-code`, `github-actions[bot]`). 203 - `correlation_id`: Stable ID used to correlate retries and downstream events for the same logical action. 204 - `outcome`: Final attempt status. Must be exactly `success` or `failure`. 205 - `retry_count`: Number of retries attempted before final outcome (`0` for first-try success/failure). 206 - `related_ref`: URL or path to the related coordination artifact (GitHub Issue, PR, or `HANDOFF-*.md` file). 207 208 #### Enforcement Scope 209 210 - Applies to **all** MCP-mediated automation behavior implemented in: 211 - `.github/workflows/**` 212 - `.github/scripts/**` 213 - New MCP-capable workflow/script changes are non-compliant unless this template is logged for each MCP action attempt sequence. 214 215 --- 216 217 ## Sourcing Protocol 218 219 - **On the record:** Safe for public repo. All committed content is on the record. 220 - **On background:** Vault-safe but identity-protected. Use carefully — this is a public repo. 221 - **Off the record:** Ephemeral. Do not log, do not store, do not commit. If Logan says something is off the record, it does not go in files, code, comments, or commit messages. 222 223 When uncertain about sourcing category, **ask Logan**. 224 225 --- 226 227 ## Obsidian Sync / Git Boundary 228 229 Two systems share the vault. They have distinct, non-overlapping responsibilities. 230 231 | Layer | Obsidian Sync (paid) | Git / GitHub | 232 | --- | --- | --- | 233 | Plugin code (`main.js`, `manifest.json`) | Syncs across devices | Tracked — public record | 234 | Plugin settings (`data.json`) | Syncs across devices, E2E encrypted | **Gitignored** — never reaches GitHub | 235 | `community-plugins.json` | Syncs across devices | Tracked — canonical plugin list | 236 | Workspace state (`workspace.json`, `graph.json`) | Syncs across devices | Gitignored — machine-local noise | 237 | Vault content (`.md` files) | Syncs across devices | Tracked | 238 239 **Rule:** Obsidian Sync is the private courier for credentials and machine state. Git is the public record for vault content and plugin presence. The `.gitignore` wildcard `.obsidian/plugins/*/data.json` is the firewall — no `data.json` reaches GitHub without a deliberate `git add --force`. 240 241 **Required Obsidian Sync settings (enable on every device):** 242 243 - Settings → Sync → `Installed community plugins` ✓ 244 - Settings → Sync → `Plugin settings` ✓ 245 246 **Mobile:** When vault-mobile is added, Obsidian Sync carries plugins and settings automatically. Desktop-only plugins (e.g., `obsidian-local-rest-api`) will be silently skipped by mobile Obsidian. 247 248 --- 249 250 ## Git Practices 251 252 - Branch naming: 253 - `claude/description-sessionId` for Claude Code branches 254 - `copilot/description` for GitHub Copilot branches 255 - `gemini/description` for Gemini agent branches 256 - Commit messages: Clear, descriptive, explain the "why" 257 - Never force-push without explicit permission 258 - Check in before anything irreversible 259 - The legislature scraper workflow commits directly to main for automated bill updates 260 261 --- 262 263 ## Guiding Principles 264 265 - The five W's: who, what, when, where, why 266 - The four C's: collect, capture, catalogue, collate 267 - Public repo = on the record 268 - Markdown for human product. Python for machine/procedural product. 269 - Do not over-engineer. Keep it simple. Only build what's needed now. 270 - Check in before anything irreversible. 271 272 --- 273 274 ## Conversation Taxonomy 275 276 Logan uses a naming convention for AI conversations: 277 278 | Prefix | Purpose | 279 | --- | --- | 280 | PERMANENT: | Central, non-deletable conversations | 281 | PERSISTENT: | Long-running, role-specific conversations | 282 | TASK: | Bounded, completable work items | 283 | STORY: | Journalism story development | 284 | PROJECT: | Multi-session projects | 285 | ISSUE: | Problem resolution | 286 | INQUIRY: | Research questions | 287 288 --- 289 290 ## Swarm Coordination 291 292 All agents coordinate through THE COURTROOM: `!/!/!/! The world is quiet here/DOCKET.md` 293 294 That file is the live status board. Read it to orient. Update it when you start or finish work. 295 296 Task assignment flows through GitHub Issues (with `agent:*` labels) and Linear (SWARM label). Slack carries breadcrumbs. The vault is the record. 297 298 --- 299 300 ## Vault ↔ Linear Operating Model Mapping 301 302 Use this mapping to decide where work should live and what should remain ephemeral. 303 304 | Layer | Vault Role | Linear Role | Ephemeral Chat/Slack Role | 305 | --- | --- | --- | --- | 306 | **Core / Mind** (governing doctrine) | Canonical source of truth for doctrine and constraints (`CONSTITUTION.md`, `PROTOCOL.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `VAULT-CONVENTIONS.md`, `VAULT-ZONES.md`, `DECISIONS.md`) | Reference only in issue/project descriptions as links; do not duplicate doctrine text in Linear | Clarify intent in discussion, but final doctrine decisions must be written to vault governance files | 307 | **Periphery / Body** (operating mechanics) | Durable playbooks, runbooks, workflows, scripts, and system notes that define *how work is executed* | Active execution layer: projects, issues, milestones, priorities, assignees, and status tracking | Fast coordination, blockers, and quick decisions during execution; summarize durable outcomes back into vault or Linear | 308 | **Ghost / Soul** (interpretive/cultural layer) | Long-form narrative context, principles-in-practice, interpretive guidance, retrospectives that should persist | Lightweight pointers (issue comments/docs) to related vault narrative notes when relevant to work | Sensemaking, live interpretation, tone checks, brainstorming; keep ephemeral unless it becomes a durable principle | 309 | **`!` spaces** (operational infrastructure) | System control plane: DOCKET, handoffs, LEVELSETs, MCP plans, audits, routing files, and machine-operational records | Coordination mirror for assignment and accountability (SWARM-labeled issues) | Notifications/breadcrumbs only; do not treat Slack as system-of-record for infra state | 310 311 ### Decision Rule 312 313 - **Vault:** Canonical memory and doctrine (what is true, why it is true, and durable operating knowledge). 314 - **Linear:** Work management and execution state (what is being done, by whom, and current status). 315 - **Chat/Slack:** Ephemeral coordination (what is being discussed right now). 316 317 If a decision must be recoverable in six months, store it in the vault. If it needs owner + due date + status, track it in Linear. If it is transient discussion, keep it in chat/Slack. 318 Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create a summary report of my findings:

## Summary: IDAHO-VAULT Repository Exploration

### 1. `.qodo` Directory Contents
